core_intuitions: Every eval is a chain of proxies: each hop from 'is this model useful?' down to 'cross-entropy on the next token' leaks a bit of validity, so the number you read is several proxies removed from what you care about.
A good evaluator must be valid, reliable, and sensitive at once; real benchmarks routinely fail at least one, and knowing which one is failing tells you how to read the score.
Benchmark scores carry sampling noise: SE = sqrt(p(1-p)/n), so sub-1% gaps on MMLU-sized sets sit inside overlapping confidence intervals and are not real differences.

A score is only informative inside a band (~20-80%): a result far below random chance means a broken answer extractor rather than a weak model, and a result above ~80% means saturation where question quality, not capability, drives the number.
Contamination is a spectrum, not a switch — verbatim, near-verbatim, topic exposure, format priming — and cheap n-gram/canary detection only catches the top of that spectrum.
High benchmark scores are necessary but not sufficient for usefulness: distribution shift, capability-not-equal-behavior, and Goodhart's Law all open a gap once a benchmark becomes a target.

new_figures.name: eval-proxy-gap-ladder
new_figures.concept: The objective mismatch as a top-down chain of proxies, with a labeled validity gap opening at every hop.
new_figures.rationale: This is the framing thesis of the whole chapter — 'each arrow introduces a gap.' The chapter renders it only as a flat ASCII arrow list, which shows the sequence but not the crucial idea that each hop LOSES something and that the loss compounds. A single infographic that makes the widening gap visible turns the chapter's central abstraction into an image the reader carries into every later section (contamination, saturation, MC pitfalls are all instances of one of these gaps).
new_figures.brief: A vertical, top-to-bottom ladder of four stacked panels representing the chain of proxies, drawn as a funnel that narrows slightly downward to signal that each proxy captures less of the thing above it. Panels, top to bottom: (1) 'TRUE GOAL — Is this model useful for my tasks?' (widest, an open real-world/task icon); (2) 'OPERATIONALIZATION — Does it score well on a benchmark suite?'; (3) 'MEASUREMENT — MC accuracy / code pass-rate / human judgment'; (4) 'TRAINING SIGNAL — cross-entropy loss on the next token' (narrowest, a tiny token/dice icon). Between each adjacent pair, draw a downward arrow crossing a jagged 'gap' band annotated with what is lost at that hop: gap 1 'benchmarks are a fixed sample -> coverage & distribution shift'; gap 2 'a score is not the capability -> guessing, position/format artifacts'; gap 3 'the optimizer never sees the goal -> it only minimizes a proxy loss'. Show the arrows pointing only downward (the signal flows down, the goal never flows back up). Add a short side caption: 'each hop is where validity leaks; the whole chapter is a tour of these three gaps.' Reading order strictly top-to-bottom. Theme-safe: use currentColor / theme tokens and low-opacity fills for the gap bands, no hardcoded primary colors; no numbers at all in this figure.

new_figures.name: benchmark-score-informative-band
new_figures.concept: A single benchmark-score number line that unifies floor effects, random chance, the informative 20-80% band, saturation, and confidence-interval overlap.
new_figures.rationale: Three separate sections (reliability/SE, saturation, floor effects) all live on the same hidden axis: the benchmark score line. Putting them on one axis is the most practical literacy tool in the chapter — it lets a reader instantly classify any reported score as 'broken extractor', 'noise floor', 'informative', or 'saturated', and see why two frontier numbers can differ yet be statistically identical. No single frame in the text ties these together; this figure is the quantitative backbone of the chapter.
new_figures.brief: A horizontal axis from 0% to 100% labeled 'benchmark score', with four contiguous shaded zones and clear labels above/below. Zones, left to right: (a) 0% to the chance line — shaded and labeled BELOW near a dashed vertical 'random chance ~25% (4-choice)' line: 'below chance = broken answer extractor / unparsed outputs, NOT a weak model'; (b) chance line up to 20% — thin 'noise floor' sliver; (c) 20%-80% — the visually dominant highlighted band labeled 'INFORMATIVE BAND: scores track real capability'; (d) 80%-100% — shaded 'SATURATION / CEILING: gaps dominated by question quality & noise'. In the saturation zone, place two small round markers, 'Model A' and 'Model B', very close together, each with a horizontal error bar (a +/- confidence interval) whose bars clearly OVERLAP; a callout points at the overlap: 'difference < CI -> within noise'. Below the axis, add one thin comparison element: a short error bar labeled 'full set (n large)' versus a noticeably longer one labeled 'small subset (n small)' to show the CI widens on smaller sets. The ONLY numbers permitted are the structural thresholds already in the chapter (25% chance, 20%, 80%); do NOT put any invented benchmark result numbers on the model markers — they are generic A/B dots. Theme-safe: express zone shading as low-opacity fills over theme tokens (informative band slightly emphasized), dashed chance line in currentColor; must read correctly in both light and dark. Everything inside the viewBox, no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: contamination-severity-gradient
new_figures.concept: Contamination as a four-rung severity spectrum, with detectability by cheap n-gram/canary methods falling off as severity decreases.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's key contamination insight is that it is 'not binary' and that 13-gram overlap 'catches the obvious cases but misses the subtler ones.' A staircase that pairs the severity rungs with a detectability axis makes the honest-eval caveat visual: the cheap detector's reach stops well above the bottom of the spectrum. This is exactly the intuition a practitioner needs to distrust a 'decontaminated' claim.
new_figures.brief: A vertical staircase of four rungs, most-severe at the TOP descending to least-severe at the bottom: (1) 'Verbatim memorization — exact training match; model retrieves the answer, zero reasoning'; (2) 'Near-verbatim — slightly altered copy; small generalization still needed'; (3) 'Topic exposure — domain/reasoning pattern seen, not the item; largely unavoidable & arguably acceptable'; (4) 'Format priming — model saw the benchmark SHAPE (e.g. 4-choice Q&A); advantage unrelated to the capability measured'. Flank the staircase with two parallel annotation axes: on the LEFT a downward arrow 'reasoning still required: none -> some' (increasing as you descend); on the RIGHT a downward arrow 'detectability by 13-gram / canary overlap: easy to catch -> effectively invisible'. Draw a vertical bracket on the right spanning ONLY rungs 1-2, labeled 'what n-gram + canary detection actually reaches', leaving rungs 3-4 visibly outside the bracket, with a caption: 'a 13-gram-clean score can still be topic-exposed or format-primed.' Reading order top-to-bottom. Theme-safe: encode severity as an opacity gradient over a single theme token (darkest/most-saturated at top), not as red/green hues; all text legible in light and dark; no numbers except the '13-gram' label.

summary: This concept-rich chapter currently has zero figures. I propose three static infographics, no animation needed, each carrying one load-bearing intuition that the prose states but never draws. (1) eval-proxy-gap-ladder visualizes the chapter's framing thesis — the chain from 'is it useful?' down to cross-entropy loss, with a widening validity gap at every hop — replacing a flat ASCII arrow list that shows sequence but not loss. (2) benchmark-score-informative-band puts floor effects, random chance, the 20-80% informative band, saturation, and confidence-interval overlap on one shared score axis, turning three scattered sections into a single practical literacy tool for classifying any reported number. (3) contamination-severity-gradient renders contamination as a four-rung spectrum paired with a detectability axis, making visual the key caveat that cheap n-gram/canary detection only reaches the top of the spectrum. All figures are theme-safe, use only the structural integers already in the text (25/20/80, '13-gram'), and invent no benchmark results.
